publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2023-02-07 23:11:10 UTC.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Harden Franklin Taylor papers
did.abstract: Included are correspondence, clippings, notes, and published and printed materials relating to such topics as the fishing industry, frozen food technology, and his assessment of the contribution of Clarence Birdseye to the development of frozen food technology.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524rareref@cornell.eduhttp://rmc.library.cornell.edu

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Included are correspondence, clippings, notes, and published and printed materials relating to such topics as the fishing industry, frozen food technology, and his assessment of the contribution of Clarence Birdseye to the development of frozen food technology.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Harden Franklin Taylor papers, #2567. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

unittitle.extref: Link to a copy of the finding aid. (PDF file)

did.unittitle: Series I. Miscellaneous Correspondence
Taylor to Hon. Frank Oliver

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (3 pp. typescript carbon copy) Congressman from the Bronx, concerning a claim the writer (unsigned, but Taylor) had against the federal government for services rendered in connection with a publication on fish refrigeration and comment by H.F.T.

did.unittitle: Unsigned to S. A. Goldblith and B. E. Proctor

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (1 p. typescript carbon copy) (Authors "From Test Tube to Table," Technology " Review, March 1958), M.I.T., Cambridge, Massachusetts, concerning an alleged misstatement in the above noted paper concerning the origin of the frozen food industry; recipients are referred to Harden F. Taylor's study, "The Refrigeration of Fish," Appendix VIII to Report of the U.S. Commissioner of Fisheries for 1926, p. 501-633 (1927). Unsigned, but may be L. V. Burton (see below).

did.unittitle: L. V. Burton to Miss Nan Ickeringill

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Miss Nan Ickeringill is the New York Times Food Editor. On the contributions of Taylor and Birdseye. Refuting notion that Birdseye the father of frozen food industry, writer states that he believes that Birdseye was "the victim of a plausible legend that grew so large he feared to deny it," that C.B. failed in the frozen fish business in New York City, then chanced to meet Taylor who "told him what he ought to have done," and that later C.B. became associated with General Sea Foods Corporation in Gloucester, Massachusetts. Suggests recipient get the correct story of the origin of quick freezing from Taylor. "He has all the documentary evidence you may want. I should warn you however that he does not claim priority for himself, but ascribes it to Plank, Erenbaum & Reuter in Germany, many years ago." (see clipping folder).

did.unittitle: Taylor to William G. Wing

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William G. Wing is from the New York Herald Tribune. (1 p. typescript carbon copy) On Wing's article on New England fishing industry.

did.unittitle: "L.V.B." to J. F. Thomas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (1 p. typescript carbon copy) Concerning this writer's article in The American Weekly; states "I don't know how it comes about that all you feature writers on food topics persist in giving credit to Clarence Birdseye. . . he did a lot to popularize food freezing, but. . .did not originate the method, nor was he the first to preserve foods commercially by freezing." Goes on to suggest he contact Taylor, ". . .who is the one who gave - Clarence Birdseye his first successful idea." (see below for American Weekly feature in question).

did.unittitle: Series II. Information Regarding Mammoth Carcasses and Related Correspondence
H. E. Anthony to Taylor

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: H. E. Anthony is the Curator, Department of Mammalogy, The American Museum of Natural History, New York City. (1 p. typescript) Re: edibility of the flesh of mammoths which have been preserved in ice or frozen ground.

did.unittitle: Richard S. Lull to Taylor

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Richard S. Lull is the Director, Peabody Museum of Natural History, Yale. (1 p. typescript) Referring him to certain studies on mammoth collecting in Siberia and discussing the edibility of mammoth flesh.

did.unittitle: William R. Farrand, "Frozen Mammoths and Modern Geology,"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Clipped from Science, Vol. 133, No. 3455, pp. 729-735.

did.unittitle: Extracts from travel accounts, scientific journals , etc.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: With comments of compiler, presumably Taylor. 13 pp. typescript.

did.unittitle: Series III. Biographical and Bibliographical Information on Taylor
Biographical outline

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1 p. Typescript on heavy paper

did.unittitle: List of publications

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 pp. Typescript on heavy paper.

did.unittitle: List of publications

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 pp. Typescript carbon.

did.unittitle: Department of Commerce Fisheries Service Bulletin, Washington

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: No. 104; Contains news note on the resignation of Taylor from the post of assistant in charge of the fisheries industries division

did.unittitle: Selected pages from various lists of Bureau of Fisheries publications, with those of Taylor checked

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 5 pcs.

did.unittitle: One photograph of Taylor and another man.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (approx. 4 1/4" w., 3 1/3" h.) Autographed on reverse: "With many thanks Yours Trochkintz [spelling uncertain]

did.unittitle: Series IV. Monographs by Taylor
"Brine Freezing of Fish"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dept. of Commerce Bureau of Fisheries, Economic Circular No. 53, 8 pp. (2 copies)

did.unittitle: "Refrigeration in the Preservation of Food."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (Paper No. 319). Notation in pencil, "World Engineering Congress Tokyo, Japan. 9 pp. + 3 pp. illus.

did.unittitle: Proceedings of the Divisional Meetings of the Institute of American Meat Packers Held at Chicago, February 29, 1930. and at New York, March 6, 1930

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Issued by the Institute: Chicago 71 pp. Includes paper by Taylor, "Our Experiences with Quick Freezing Methods," 17-44.

did.unittitle: Series V. Patent Papers
Taylor's packaging (mainly for frozen fish fillets) patent.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4 Xerox pp. (3 pcs.). No information as to location of original.

did.unittitle: Taylor's patent, for process of treating comestibles

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ("Particularly to treating fish, meats, poultry, etc. to improve their quality and prevent certain undesirable changes which may occur, especially while the foods are frozen or during storage in the frozen condition.") 4 pp. negative photostat with New York Public Library stamp

did.unittitle: Series VI. Origin or 'Invention' of Quick Freezing as Represented in the Popular Press
Quotations and Excerpts from Newspapers and Magazines

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 15 pp. Typescript carbon + title p. Note attached: "This exhibit was not submitted with the MS of the New Orleans Meeting. H.F.T."

did.unittitle: Series VII. Magazine Article reprints or clippings
R. Heiss, "Gegenwartsfragen der Konservierung von Lebensmittelr. durch Kalte,"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Zeitschrift des Vereines Deutscher Ingenieure, pp. 1145-1154

did.unittitle: Harold T. Meryman, "Mechanics of Freezing in Living Cells and Tissues,"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Science, Vol. 124, No. 3221, pp. 515-521. Also a commentary on this paper, "Mechanism of Freezing in (Plant. or Animal?) Living Cells and Tissues," by J. Levitt, Science, February 1, 1957. p. 194

did.unittitle: Series VIII. Clarence Birdseye, 1886-1956. Clippings and Notes Apparently Collected by H.F.T.
Article on Birdseye

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Clipped from Newsweek, pp. 72-73.

did.unittitle: Don Wharton,"'Birdseye' Also Means a Man"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reader's Digest, pp. 71-74.

did.unittitle: Fragment beginning "The industry accepts July 31, 1923, as its birthday. It was then that Clarence Birdseye. . .started the first fish-freezing plant in New York. . ."
Note on Birdseye in "The Periscope" column

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Newsweek, p.18

did.unittitle: Obituaries

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From the Times, Tribune, World Telegram & Sun, Journal-American, and Wall Street Journal. 5 items.

did.unittitle: Article on Birdseye,

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Clipped from Time, pp. 86, 88-89

did.unittitle: Brief section on Birdseye

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In The American Weekly, Sunday supplement of The Journal American, p. 9.

did.unittitle: Miscellaneous notes on Birdseye and his enterprises

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written in part on 3x5's, apparently by H.F.T. 11 items.

did.unittitle: Series IX. Miscellaneous Publications
2 pp. clipped from Moody's Manual of Investments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: General Foods Corporation (p. 3014) and Frosted Food Company (p. 3015).

did.unittitle: John A. Zapp, Jr., "The Anatomy of a Rumor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 14 pp. pamphlet defending the use of Teflon in cooking utensils.

did.unittitle: Bulletin, Silicate P's & Q's

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Vol. 45, No. 5, by Philadelphia Quartz Company. 2 pp. (1 pc.)

did.unittitle: Newspaper clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 22 items, mainly from the Times and Tribune, concerning the frozen food industry, fisheries, and various persons and subjects of interest to H.F.T.

did.unittitle: Bibliographical references and miscellaneous notes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 10 items
